passages.explanation: Peter and John go to the temple at the afternoon prayer hour. A man who's been lame from birth is carried there daily to beg at the Beautiful Gate. He asks them for money. Peter looks at him directly and says he has no silver or gold, but what he does have he'll give: in the name of Jesus Christ, get up and walk. Peter takes his hand, pulls him up, and instantly his feet and ankles become strong. The man leaps up, walks, and enters the temple jumping and praising God.

Everyone recognises the man — he's the beggar who sat at the Beautiful Gate every day. They're filled with wonder and astonishment. The healed man clings to Peter and John, and all the people come running to them at Solomon's Porch. The miracle is undeniable because everyone knows this man and his condition.

Peter sees the crowd gathering and immediately redirects their amazement. Why are you staring at us, as if we made this man walk by our own power or godliness? It's the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob who has glorified his servant Jesus — the one they handed over and rejected before Pilate, even though Pilate wanted to release him. They killed the Author of Life, but God raised him from the dead. Peter and John are witnesses. It's faith in Jesus' name that healed this man.

Peter softens his tone: he knows they acted in ignorance, as did their rulers. God had announced through all the prophets that the Messiah would suffer, and this is how it was fulfilled. Now he calls them to repent and turn back, so their sins can be wiped away and times of refreshing can come from the Lord. God will send Jesus back — but heaven must receive him until the time of restoration that God spoke about through the prophets.

Peter quotes Moses, who said God would raise up a prophet like himself from among the people, and everyone who doesn't listen to that prophet will be cut off. He says all the prophets from Samuel onward pointed to these days. The listeners are heirs of the prophets and of the covenant God made with Abraham — that through his offspring all families of the earth would be blessed. God raised up his servant Jesus and sent him first to them, to bless them by turning each of them from their wickedness. The appeal is direct: this is your story, your covenant, your Messiah.
